title: West African Maafe (Peanut Stew)

subs.name: Bone-in mutton for a richer stew; increase braising time to 45 minutes. Or use paneer cubes and vegetable stock for a vegetarian version

ingredients.name: roasted peanuts, very finely ground (or 4 tbsp smooth peanut butter)
visual_checks: Peanut butter is a quicker substitute; grind with 100 ml water into a paste
ingredients.notes: Peanut butter is a quicker substitute; grind with 100 ml water into a paste

subs.name: Almond butter or cashew butter for a nut-butter variation; or toasted sunflower seeds blended smooth for a peanut-free alternative
Extra 2 tbsp blended fresh tomato, reduced for 5 additional minutes in the pot

ingredients.name: potato, peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks
visual_checks: Sweet potato is the traditional West African choice; regular potato works
ingredients.notes: Sweet potato is the traditional West African choice; regular potato works

subs.name: Sweet potato (shakarkand) for an authentically West African sweetness; add in the same braising step

steps.text: If using whole roasted peanuts, grind them in a blender with 100 ml water into a thick smooth paste. If using peanut butter, stir 4 tbsp with 100 ml warm water until smooth and lump-free. Set the peanut paste aside.
Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high flame. Season chicken pieces with salt and sear in batches for 3 minutes per side until golden on the surface. Remove and set aside.

In the same pot add onion and cook for 4 minutes until soft. Add garlic and ginger; stir for 1 minute. Add tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes until it darkens. Add fresh tomato, chilli powder, cumin, coriander powder and bay leaf; cook for 5 minutes until the tomatoes break down.

Pour in 400 ml water and return the seared chicken to the pot. Add potato chunks. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover and cook for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the potato is nearly tender.

Stir the peanut paste into the stew — the sauce will thicken immediately; stir well to fully incorporate. Add a splash of water if it becomes too thick. Simmer uncovered for 8 minutes, stirring often to prevent the peanut base sticking to the bottom. Taste for salt and remove the bay leaf.
The finished maafe should be a rich terracotta-coloured stew with a thick peanut sauce clinging to each piece of chicken. Serve hot over plain white rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: peanut → Almond butter or cashew butter for a nut-butter variation; or toasted sunflower seeds blended smooth for a peanut-free alternative; chicken → Bone-in mutton for a richer stew; increase braising time to 45 minutes. Or use paneer cubes and vegetable stock for a vegetarian version; potato → Sweet potato (shakarkand) for an authentically West African sweetness; add in the same braising step; tomato-paste → Extra 2 tbsp blended fresh tomato, reduced for 5 additional minutes in the pot
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Peanuts (moongphali / verkadalai) are widely sold roasted in Indian markets. Grind them in a mixie with water to make the paste — identical to making peanut chutney. All spices are from the standard Indian pantry and the braising technique mirrors Indian chicken curry. Cook in a kadai or pressure cooker (3 whistles on step 4, then add peanut paste and finish on the stovetop).
